findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-process warn-deduplication mechanism (e.g. a module-level `_TOMBSTONE_WARNED: set[str]` checked before emitting each `logging.warning` for a warn-class tombstone, cleared only on cache invalidation) so repeated `load_config()` calls in a long-running MCP server do not produce unbounded identical log entries.
evidence: 'Place the env-var tombstone check at the top of load_config(), before the cache lookup, so it fires unconditionally'
AC: 'Operationally-inert retired inputs … warn rather than error — the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger)'
No mention of deduplication, once-per-process guard, or `_WARNED` set anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: Warn-only tombstone checks (`behavior='warn'`) run unconditionally at the top of `load_config()` before the `_RESULT_CACHE` lookup on every invocation. In a long-running MCP server where a retired warn-class env var (e.g. `REBAR_PUSH`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`) remains set in the deployment environment, `logging.warning` fires on every request/tool call with no per-process deduplication. The plan specifies no `_WARNED: set[str]` guard or equivalent once-per-process mechanism. This produces unbounded log accumulation that can mask real operational issues and makes log-based alerting unreliable.
findings.location: What section — 'Cache-safe placement (important)' + Scope: `src/rebar/config.py`; AC bullet 3 (warn-only behavior)
scenarios: Long-running rebar-mcp server with `REBAR_PUSH` set in environment: every MCP tool call triggers `load_config()`, which emits a warning log entry before the cache hit, producing thousands of identical warning lines per session.
Log-based alerting on `rebar` logger WARNING level fires continuously, masking real warnings about new issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_TOMBSTONE_WARNED: set[str] = set()` at module level in `config.py`. In the pre-cache warn path: `if name not in _TOMBSTONE_WARNED: logging.warning(...); _TOMBSTONE_WARNED.add(name)`. Clear `_TOMBSTONE_WARNED` entries when the corresponding env var disappears (env-signature change → cache miss → re-check). This matches the `_WARNED` pattern common in deprecation frameworks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `except RemovedInputError: raise` re-raise guards in `mcp_server.py` log the error before re-raising (e.g. `logger.error('Retired config input detected: %s', e); raise`) so the failure is observable in structured MCP server logs, not only as a propagated exception frame.

AC: 'verified by an in-process test that … asserts RemovedInputError propagates out'
No mention of logging/instrumentation before the re-raise in the MCP path; observability of this new failure point is unspecified
findings.finding: The plan specifies clean stderr output for the CLI path when `RemovedInputError` propagates, but specifies NO structured logging or instrumentation before the `BaseException` propagates out of the MCP server request/startup path. When the MCP server refuses a write request or fails startup due to a tombstoned input, there is no specified `logger.error(...)` or `logger.critical(...)` call before re-raise at `mcp_server.py:302-312`. Operators of a persistent MCP server get no structured log entry attributing the crash/refusal to a retired config input — only a raw Python traceback if the exception reaches an unhandled boundary.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' + 'MCP-server exit semantics'; Scope: MCP server entry path; AC bullet 5
scenarios: rebar-mcp server running as a background process with stdout/stderr not connected to a terminal: `RemovedInputError` propagates silently through the async event loop without a log entry, leaving operators with no trace of why the server stopped accepting write requests.
SRE investigating a rebar-mcp incident has no structured log record to search; the only signal is the absence of successful tool calls.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the re-raise guard pattern from `except RemovedInputError: raise` to `except RemovedInputError as _rmi: logger.error('Retired config input rejected: %s', _rmi); raise` at each site in `mcp_server.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior of `rebar config validate`/`doctor` when TOML is malformed or unreadable: document whether `scan_tombstones` receives a partial/empty parse and degrades gracefully, or whether the caller catches the TOML parse error and emits a distinct 'config file unreadable — fix parse errors first' message before exiting non-zero (entry point, both failure exits, and clean-environment success exit must all be covered in the AC).

'walks the registry and returns all matches without raising' — only specifies behavior on a successfully-parsed config, not on a parse error

findings.location: Scope — 'New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor)' section; AC: 'rebar config validate / rebar doctor uses scan_tombstones(…)'
scenarios: Operator has a typo in rebar.toml and also a stale TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR set; they run `rebar config validate` to diagnose — the command may throw an unhandled TOML parse exception instead of reporting either problem.

CI pipeline runs `rebar config validate` as a pre-flight check; a branch introduces a TOML syntax error; the command aborts with a raw traceback rather than a clean non-zero exit with a parse-error message.

Update the `scan_tombstones` signature doc to specify `toml_tables: dict | None` and define the None-safe behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a test case) for `rebar config validate` when the config file contains malformed TOML: specify that the command catches the TOML parse error, emits a structured error message to stderr naming the file and parse error, and exits non-zero — so the malformed-TOML failure path has a concrete, observable contract like the retired-input path does.
evidence: "uses `scan_tombstones(...)` to walk the registry against env + TOML + a leftover flat `.rebar/config.conf` directly (NOT the raising `load_config()` path)"
"verified by a test asserting `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR=old rebar config validate` exits non-zero with that report while a clean environment exits 0"

findings.finding: The `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command explicitly bypasses `load_config()` and must parse TOML itself before passing `toml_tables` to `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths)`. The plan's ACs for this command cover only two scenarios: a retired error-class input present (exits non-zero) and a clean environment (exits 0). No AC or test addresses what the command does when a config file exists but contains malformed TOML — an invalid/error-input path for a new user-facing flow. Because the command explicitly divorces itself from `load_config()`'s TOML-parse error handling, this gap is not covered by the existing config error path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` AC; Scope — `scan_tombstones` function
scenarios: A developer runs `rebar config validate` on a repo whose `rebar.toml` has a TOML syntax error; the command's TOML parsing step throws an unspecified exception; the user sees a raw traceback rather than a structured diagnostic, and the exit code and message format are undefined.
An operator uses `rebar doctor` in a CI gate; a bad merge introduces a TOML syntax error; the gate exits with an unexpected code/format, confusing the diagnosis.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `rebar config validate` AC: 'When TOML parsing of any config file fails, the command catches the parse exception, writes `<filename>: TOML parse error: <detail>` to stderr, and exits non-zero — verified by a test that passes a file containing invalid TOML to the validate command and asserts exit-code 1 and the error message format.' In the Scope section, note that the `rebar config validate` wiring in `show_config.py` wraps the TOML parse step in a `try/except tomllib.TOMLDecodeError` (or equivalent) before calling `scan_tombstones`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow AC7's grep-based guard to the specific known swallow-sites (mcp_server.py and llm/config.py tracker-dir handler) rather than asserting every `except Exception` in the codebase has a re-raise guard — the 130+ unrelated broad-catch handlers across llm/, grounding/, and reconciler/ make a codebase-wide check unworkable.
evidence: AC7: 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it'

Search results: 130+ `except Exception` hits across src/rebar/llm/, src/rebar/grounding/, src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/, src/rebar/_snapshot/, src/rebar/llm/plan_review/, etc. — none of which are on the config-load path

The plan itself identifies only mcp_server.py:302-312 and :311 as the two handlers that need guarding — yet the AC check would scan all `except Exception` in the entire codebase, not just mcp_server.py
findings.finding: AC7's proposed pytest guard — asserting that EVERY line in the source matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it — is over-engineered and unworkable. The codebase has 130+ `except Exception` sites across `llm/`, `grounding/`, `reconciler/`, `_snapshot/`, etc., that are entirely unrelated to config loading. Requiring a re-raise guard on all of them would create ~120+ spurious failures and an unmaintainable test. The actual protection needed is narrow: confirm the specific MCP boot handler (`mcp_server.py:302-312`) and the `llm/config.py:373` tracker-dir handler re-raise `RemovedInputError`. findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit every error-class env var (`REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`, and any others seeded as env/error) for dedicated readers that bypass `load_config()` (pattern: direct `os.environ.get(...)` calls outside `load_config()`/`env_overrides()`), and place each tombstone check at that reader's source — not only at the top of `load_config()` — following the same rationale applied to `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`.
evidence: Plan explicitly states: 'A load_config()-only check would leave those callers reading the wrong store silently. So the TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR env-var tombstone check is placed inside tracker_dir_override() (the single source for that env read)'

Plan then states for other env vars: 'the top-of-load_config() scan still covers the other error-class env vars (REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, …)' — but provides NO audit evidence that REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE lacks a similar dedicated reader
The plan's own logic proves the risk: if TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR needed this bypass audit, so does every error-class env var on a security-sensitive path
ABSENCE: no statement that the codebase was searched for all direct os.environ reads of the named error-class env vars outside load_config()
findings.finding: The plan audits whether `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` has a bypassing dedicated reader (`tracker_dir_override()`) and places its tombstone there, but does NOT confirm the same audit was performed for the other error-class env vars (`REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`, etc.) on the MCP write-gate path. If any of those vars are also read via a function that bypasses `load_config()`, placing their tombstone only at the top of `load_config()` would leave the MCP write-gate silently unprotected on that bypass path — the exact failure mode the plan is designed to prevent.
findings.location: What section — 'TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR is gated at the single env-read source' + Scope: config.py; Seed registry: env error entries
scenarios: An operator sets REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE=1 in a stale manifest; if that var is read by a dedicated function (like tracker_dir_override for TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR), the top-of-load_config tombstone never fires, the write gate silently reverts to permissive, and live Jira sync executes unguarded
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope / What section: 'Before placement, grep the codebase for `os.environ` reads of each error-class env var (REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, …) outside config.py's load_config()/env_overrides(); any dedicated reader found receives its own inline tombstone check, superseding the load_config() placement for that var.' Document the audit result (found / not found) explicitly in the plan so implementers have confirmation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact emission mechanism for warn-class tombstones: either define a new `warn_tombstone(key)` helper (parallel to `warn_deprecated`) that looks up `TOMBSTONE_REGISTRY` and emits via `logger.warning`, making it exempt from the alias-completeness scan, or document that warn-class tombstones require a parallel `_ENTRIES` row and explain how the two registries stay separated.
evidence: Plan: 'the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger, the same channel warn_deprecated uses)'
Plan: '_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIORS = frozenset({"error", "warn"})' — separate from _KINDS/REGISTRY
Code: `warn_deprecated` at _deprecations.py:132 does `dep = REGISTRY[key]  # KeyError on an unregistered surface — intentional (completeness)`
Code: test_deprecation_registry.py comment in _deprecations.py module docstring: 'source-scans for raw `is deprecated` / `DeprecationWarning` emissions bypassing this helper'
findings.finding: The warn-class tombstone emission path is unspecified relative to the existing `warn_deprecated` / REGISTRY completeness guard: `warn_deprecated` raises `KeyError` for any key not in `REGISTRY`, and the test in `test_deprecation_registry.py` source-scans for raw deprecation emissions that bypass the helper. The plan says warn-class tombstones emit 'via the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger, the same channel warn_deprecated uses)' but does not specify whether this routes through `warn_deprecated` (requiring a parallel entry in `_ENTRIES` / `REGISTRY`) or through a new direct `logger.warning` call. If the latter, the existing completeness test will flag it as a bypass; if the former, the tombstone and alias registries are conflated despite the plan saying to keep them separate.
findings.location: What section — 'Warn-only for operationally inert retired inputs'; Scope — `src/rebar/_deprecations.py`; AC — 'Operationally-inert retired inputs warn rather than error'
scenarios: A warn-class tombstone for REBAR_PUSH emits logger.warning directly → test_deprecation_registry completeness scan flags it as an unauthorized bypass → CI red
A warn-class tombstone routes through warn_deprecated → requires a REGISTRY entry → the tombstone and alias registries are effectively merged, contradicting 'do not overload it'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `warn_tombstone(removed_input: RemovedInput, *, logger=None)` function in `_deprecations.py` that emits via `(logger or _DEFAULT_LOGGER).warning(...)` directly from the tombstone registry (not REGISTRY), so it is distinct from `warn_deprecated` and the completeness scan's scope is unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `file_paths` parameter semantics in the `scan_tombstones` protocol: specify whether it is (a) a sequence of `Path` objects that `scan_tombstones` probes with `.exists()` itself, or (b) a pre-computed set of present file paths the caller resolves — and document this in the Scope section alongside the function signature.
evidence: Plan: 'the file-presence tombstone (`.rebar/config.conf`) is likewise an unconditional check at the top of `load_config()` before the cache-key computation — `if (repo_root(root) / '.rebar' / 'config.conf').exists(): raise RemovedInputError(...)` — NOT added to `_config_probe_paths()`'
Plan: '`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` uses `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]` that walks the registry and returns all matches without raising'

ABSENCE: the `file_paths` parameter type/semantics are never defined — is it a list of Paths to probe with `.exists()`, a list of already-read file contents, or a list of already-detected present files?
findings.finding: `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths)` is referenced with a signature but its decision protocol for the `'file'` kind is ambiguous: the plan also says the file-presence check is 'an unconditional check at the top of `load_config()` before the cache-key computation' via `if (repo_root(root) / '.rebar' / 'config.conf').exists(): raise`. These are two different execution paths — one non-raising scan via `file_paths`, one raising check at load_config() — but no protocol specifies whether `scan_tombstones` does a live `Path.exists()` probe or uses the caller-provided `file_paths` argument, which matters for the `rebar config validate` use case (it must not call `load_config()`) and for testability.
findings.location: What section — 'Cache-safe placement'; Scope — 'New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor)'; AC — '`rebar config validate` uses `scan_tombstones(...)` to walk the registry against env + TOML + a leftover flat `.rebar/config.conf` directly (NOT the raising `load_config()` path)'
scenarios: An implementer passes `file_paths=[]` to scan_tombstones from `config validate` → file tombstones are never detected → the validate command misses the .rebar/config.conf tombstone silently
An implementer does a live `Path.exists()` inside scan_tombstones → the function has a side effect not visible from the signature → testing requires a real filesystem

findings.finding: The plan's MCP `except Exception` re-raise audit covers only the two named blocks at `mcp_server.py:302-312/:311`, but the codebase has additional `except Exception` blocks in write-tool code paths reachable from MCP tools: `_mcp_writes.py:288` (workflow load error), `:322` (background run failure), `:327` (failure-recording path). A `RemovedInputError` raised inside `tracker_dir()` or `load_config()` during an MCP write tool invocation would travel through these `_mcp_writes.py` handlers, not just the boot-time `mcp_server.py` ones. The plan says 'Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare `except:` / `except BaseException` swallows it' but does not name the `_mcp_writes.py` handlers or require them to be audited.

findings.finding: The plan places the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check at the top of `load_config()`, but `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:_resolve_tracker_dir` (line 171-182) calls `tracker_dir_override()` directly — which only reads `os.environ.get('REBAR_TRACKER_DIR')` — and never calls `load_config()`. An operator running the reconciler with `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` set would bypass the tombstone entirely; the check needs to also fire at `tracker_dir_override()` or `tracker_dir()`, or the reconciler's direct call path must be enumerated as a required update site.
findings.location: What section / Scope (`src/rebar/config.py`) / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:171-182`
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check into `tracker_dir_override()` itself (or at the top of `tracker_dir()`), not just `load_config()`. Since `tracker_dir_override()` is the single source for the env read, this covers all call paths — `load_config()`, `tracker_dir()`, and `_resolve_tracker_dir` — without additional per-site changes.

findings.finding: The source-grep pattern `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` used in AC6's pytest is a substring match with no word-boundary anchor and would both false-positive on `except ExceptionGroup` or `except ExceptionHandler` and false-negative on `except (Exception, SomeError):` multi-exception tuples — making the 'reproducible check' unreliable as specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6: 'a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grep-based check with an AST-walk over `mcp_server.py` that finds all `ExceptHandler` nodes whose `type` is or includes `Exception`/`BaseException` and asserts each has a preceding sibling that re-raises `RemovedInputError` — more robust than regex on source text.

findings.finding: `pytest-socket` is referenced in AC2 as the primary network-blocking mechanism for test assertions, but it is not installed in the project's dev dependencies (pyproject.toml lists pytest, pytest-cov, pytest-xdist, pytest-split — no pytest-socket). While the plan offers `unittest.mock.patch` as a fallback in the same sentence, the AC phrasing 'enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch`' leaves `pytest-socket` as the leading option, risking a contributor adding a `pytest.mark.socket_disabled` decorator that silently has no effect.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC line 2: 'no network call (enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive asserting `call_count == 0`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC2 network-isolation clause to: 'no network call (enforced by `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive, asserting `call_count == 0`)' — removing the pytest-socket alternative to avoid the vacuous-no-op risk, since mock.patch is already available with zero new dependencies.

findings.finding: The plan's AC says to verify the MCP write path by calling 'an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)'. In the actual codebase, `create_ticket` is a nested closure defined inside `register_write_tools()` and registered on the FastMCP server object — it is not a module-level importable symbol in `_mcp_writes.py` or `mcp_server.py`. There is no `rebar.mcp_server.create_ticket` or `rebar._mcp_writes.create_ticket` to call directly in a test.
findings.location: AC: 'MCP-server exit semantics' bullet — 'verified by an in-process test that ... calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)' with 'calls `rebar.create_ticket(...)` directly (the library function the MCP tool delegates to) after priming the config cache and setting the retired env var, and asserts `RemovedInputError` propagates'.

findings.finding: The `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone is described as 'applied in the LLM config load path' (`src/rebar/llm/`) rather than registered in the central `_deprecations.py` registry, but `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths)` is described as walking 'the registry' — leaving it ambiguous whether `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` would detect a set `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`. If the LLM tombstone is only a local check in `LLMConfig.from_env()` and not in `_deprecations.py`, `scan_tombstones` silently misses it, and the validate/doctor command gives a false-all-clear to operators with that var set.
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/llm/` (`LLMConfig.from_env()`) — apply the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone there (it is not a core-`config.py` var)'; AC: '`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` uses `scan_tombstones(...)` to walk the registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Register all tombstones — including `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` — in `_deprecations.py`. In `LLMConfig.from_env()`, call the shared `check_tombstones(env)` helper (same one `load_config()` calls) rather than a local check, so the central registry is the single source of truth for both the raising path and `scan_tombstones`.
